docs.title: Abstract
docs.text:
Use this section for the final abstract shown on the website homepage.

Write one compact summary that states the problem, the study objective, the method or framework used, the key results, and the main conclusion or practical implication.

Suggested length: 150 to 300 words unless your department requires a different range.

docs.title: IMRAD Reading Flow
docs.text:
Read the study in this order:

  1. Introduction
  2. Related Literature
  3. Methods
  4. Results
  5. Discussion
  6. References
  7. Appendices

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:

docs.title: Notes for Web Publishing
docs.text:
• Put the final homepage title exactly as approved in your defense or proposal records.
  • Replace the placeholders above before publishing.
  • Keep print-only pages such as the title page, manual table of contents, and figure or table lists under Print Companion only when your department still requires them.

docs.title: References
docs.text: Use this page for the complete list of sources cited across the IMRAD manuscript.

docs.title: IEEE Reference Instructions
docs.text:
• Include only sources that are actually cited in the study.
  • Use IEEE citation format consistently for all entries.
  • Number the references in the order they first appear in the manuscript.
  • Update this page whenever a new in-text citation is added to Chapters 1 to 5 or the appendices.

docs.title: In-Text Citation Guide
docs.text:
Use bracketed reference numbers in the manuscript, for example:

  • single source: [1]
  • multiple sources: [2], [3]
  • consecutive sources: [4]-[6]

Do not use author-date citations if the manuscript is following IEEE format.

docs.title: IEEE Reference List
docs.text:
Replace the sample entries below with your actual sources.

  1. A. A. Author, "Title of journal article," Journal Title, vol. 00, no. 0, pp. 00-00, 2026, doi: 10.0000/example.
  2. B. B. Author and C. C. Author, Title of Book, 2nd ed. City, Country: Publisher, 2026.
  3. D. D. Author, "Title of conference paper," in Proceedings of the Name of Conference, City, Country, 2026, pp. 00-00.
  4. Organization Name, "Title of web page or report," Site or Publisher Name, Year. [Online]. Available: https://example.com
  5. E. E. Author, Title of Thesis. City, Country: University Name, 2026.

docs.title: In-Text Citation Check
docs.text:
Before final submission, verify the following:

  • every in-text citation has a matching entry on this page
  • every entry on this page is cited at least once in the manuscript
  • reference numbers appear in first-citation order
  • author names, publication years, titles, page numbers, and links or DOIs are accurate
  • punctuation, abbreviations, and ordering follow IEEE style consistently

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Use official IEEE formatting for the final version, especially for journal titles, conference names, edition labels, page ranges, and online sources.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Chapter 1 Introduction
docs.text: This chapter establishes the project context, the study purpose, the objectives, and the intended scope of the approved MVP.

docs.title: Included Pages
docs.text:
• Project Context
  • Purpose and Description
  • Objectives and RQ Alignment
  • Scope and Limitations

docs.title: Chapter Use
docs.text:
Use this chapter to explain why the project matters, what it intends to deliver, and which boundaries define the approved study.

Next ➡️

docs.title: Project Context
docs.text: Use this section to explain the real-world setting of the project and the concrete problem that motivated the study.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Describe the following in a concise engineering style:

  • the organization, community, or user environment where the problem occurs
  • the target users or stakeholders affected by the problem
  • the current workflow, current system, or current practice
  • the specific pain points, inefficiencies, risks, or limitations in that current condition
  • why a software-based solution is appropriate for the problem

docs.title: 1. Operational Context
docs.text: Briefly explain the domain and where the problem occurs.

docs.title: 2. Stakeholders and Current Practice
docs.text: Identify the primary users and summarize how the work is currently done.

docs.title: 3. Problem Statement Basis
docs.text: State the concrete pain points that justify the project. Use observable issues such as delays, errors, repeated manual work, poor traceability, low accessibility, or weak decision support.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Avoid broad history, generic industry background, or solution details that belong in later sections. The goal of this section is to make the problem understandable and credible.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Purpose and Description
docs.text: Use this section to explain what the proposed system is and what it is intended to do for the target users.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following:

  • the name and general type of system being proposed
  • the main users of the system
  • the primary purpose of the solution
  • the major capabilities expected from the MVP
  • the core logic or technical contribution when it is important to understanding the project direction

docs.title: 1. One-Paragraph System Overview
docs.text: Explain the system in plain technical language.

docs.title: 2. Main Users and Intended Benefit
docs.text: State who will use the system and what practical value they gain from it.

docs.title: 3. Major MVP Capabilities
docs.text: List the most important functions only. Keep the description aligned with the approved MVP rather than the full future system.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Do not overload this section with architecture, chapter summaries, or sprint details. Focus on what the system is for and what core capability it will provide.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Objectives and RQ Alignment
docs.text: Use this section to state the project objectives in a way that aligns directly with the institutional research questions.

docs.title: Required Alignment
docs.text:
The standard objective pattern is:

  • RQ1 - Requirements Engineering: analyze or define the requirements needed to address the problem
  • RQ2 - System Implementation: design and implement the MVP or core artifact
  • RQ3 - System Validation: validate the developed artifact using measurable evidence

docs.title: Suggested Format
docs.text: Objective Related RQ Notes on Measurement or Output To analyze and define the functional and non-functional requirements of the proposed system. RQ1 Requirements basis, user stories, backlog, or design inputs To design and implement an MVP that addresses the identified problem. RQ2 Architecture, core logic, implemented features To validate the developed artifact using testing, user evaluation, or other approved technical metrics. RQ3 Functional testing, SUS, SEQ, accuracy, performance, or similar evidence

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Use action verbs such as analyze, design, implement, and validate. Avoid vague objectives such as "to know," "to understand," or "to determine" unless they lead to a concrete engineering output.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Scope and Limitations
docs.text: Use this section to define the MVP boundary clearly so reviewers can distinguish what the team committed to deliver from what is intentionally excluded.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
State the following:

  • the features included in the MVP
  • the intended users, deployment setting, or coverage boundary
  • major technical, operational, or data-related constraints
  • features, integrations, or conditions that are explicitly out of scope

docs.title: 1. MVP Scope
docs.text: List the essential functions that must be present for the system to demonstrate the proposed solution.

docs.title: 2. Operational Boundaries
docs.text: State where and for whom the system applies, such as one campus, one office, one user group, or one device class.

docs.title: 3. Limitations
docs.text: Document realistic constraints such as unavailable integrations, limited training data, single-platform support, small respondent count, or prototype-only deployment.

docs.title: Optional MoSCoW Table
docs.text:
This table is only a scope-writing aid. It does not replace the product backlog, sprint planning, or GitHub issue prioritization used elsewhere in the project.

Priority Features Must Have Should Have Could Have Won't Have

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Be explicit about exclusions. A strong scope section reduces scope creep and prevents the panel from assuming features that were never approved.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Chapter 2 Related Literature
docs.text: This chapter synthesizes prior studies, comparable systems, and technical references that establish the basis for the project's design and technical gap.

docs.title: Included Pages
docs.text:
• Domain Literature
  • Synthesis Matrix
  • Technical Gap

docs.title: Chapter Use
docs.text:
Use this chapter to show how existing work informs the project and where a credible unresolved gap remains.

Next ➡️

docs.title: Domain Literature
docs.text: Use this section to review the prior studies, systems, and technical references that explain the problem space and the approaches already used in the field.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Organize the discussion by themes such as:

  • domain problem and current practice
  • comparable systems or prior implementations
  • relevant algorithms, frameworks, or architectural approaches
  • evaluation methods used in similar projects

docs.title: Writing Pattern Per Source Group
docs.text:
For each group of sources, explain:

  1. what problem or task the source addressed
  2. what method, technology, or system approach it used
  3. what strength it demonstrated
  4. what limitation or unresolved condition remains

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Use IEEE citations and write by synthesis, not by isolated article summaries. The purpose of the chapter is to build a technical basis for the project, not to collect generic definitions.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Synthesis Matrix
docs.text: Use this section to organize the strongest related studies, systems, or technical references in a comparative form before drafting the technical gap.

docs.title: Standard Matrix
docs.text: Source Problem or Domain Core Technology or Approach Primary Strength Reported Limitation or Gap Relevance to the Project

docs.title: How to Use the Matrix
docs.text:
After completing the table, summarize the recurring patterns you found, such as:

  • repeated limitations in existing systems
  • methods that perform well but are costly or difficult to deploy
  • workflow gaps that remain unaddressed in the local context
  • evaluation methods commonly used in related work

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
This matrix should support the next section on the technical gap. Select sources that directly inform the chosen project rather than filling the table with loosely related references.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Technical Gap
docs.text: Use this section to explain why the proposed project is still necessary after reviewing existing literature and comparable systems.

docs.title: 1. Current Approaches
docs.text: Summarize what existing studies or systems commonly do to address the problem.

docs.title: 2. Documented Limitation
docs.text: Explain the limitation that remains. This may be a technical, usability, deployment, performance, integration, or local-context gap.

docs.title: 3. Project Response
docs.text: State how the proposed project addresses that limitation through its architecture, workflow support, core logic, or validation strategy.

docs.title: Standard Framing Pattern
docs.text:
• Existing systems commonly use X to address Y.
  • However, prior work shows that X is limited when Z is present.
  • The proposed project addresses this by implementing A for B.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Do not claim that the project is the first of its kind unless that statement is strongly supported. A credible gap is usually a remaining limitation or unmet requirement, not total novelty.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Chapter 3 Methods
docs.text: This chapter documents the institutional framework, the Scrum planning artifacts, and the technical design choices used to build and validate the system.

docs.title: Included Pages
docs.text:
• Institutional Framework
  • Product Backlog
  • Sprint Structure
  • Definition of Done
  • System Architecture
  • Logic Flow
  • Data Schema
  • Validation Plan

docs.title: Chapter Use
docs.text:
Use this chapter to explain how the study was organized, how the artifact was implemented, and how success was evaluated.

Next ➡️

docs.title: Institutional Framework
docs.text: Use this section to explain the institutional framework that governs the project and how the team applied it in the study.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Describe the following:

  • the use of Design Science Research as the institutional research design
  • the alignment of the project to RQ1, RQ2, and RQ3
  • the use of Scrum as the development methodology for planning, implementation, review, and improvement
  • the major institutional checkpoints such as Gate 1, Gate 2, Gate 3, and Gate 4
  • the main development environment and repository tools used to carry out the approved workflow

docs.title: 1. Design Science Research
docs.text: Explain that the study focuses on defining the problem, building an artifact, and validating that artifact.

docs.title: 2. Scrum-Based Development
docs.text: Explain how the backlog, sprint structure, reviews, and retrospectives support the implementation process.

docs.title: 3. Institutional Checkpoints
docs.text: Summarize how the gates and appendix records support approval, monitoring, and final evaluation.

docs.title: 4. Development Environment and Project Controls
docs.text: Briefly identify the main tools that supported the workflow, such as the source repository, issue tracker, documentation repository, CI checks, or local development environment. This subsection should remain concise and should only explain the tools insofar as they supported the mandated institutional process.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Keep this section focused on how the framework was applied to the project. Avoid spending excessive space justifying why DSR or Scrum was chosen, because they are already institutional defaults.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Product Backlog
docs.text: Use this section to explain how the approved requirements were translated into user stories, backlog items, and sprint-ready work.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following:

  • the basis of the backlog, such as stakeholder needs, user stories, and MVP priorities
  • how backlog items were grouped into epics or functional areas
  • how priorities were assigned
  • how acceptance criteria and story points were used when applicable
  • how GitHub issues or equivalent tracking IDs were assigned to backlog items
  • how the Definition of Done was applied before a story could be treated as complete

docs.title: Backlog Traceability
docs.text:
The backlog should make it possible to trace each planned feature from user story to implementation evidence. When applicable, the final manuscript should show how each backlog item connects to:

  • a requirement, objective, or approved user story
  • an issue, card, or ticket in the project tracker
  • acceptance criteria used to verify the item
  • the sprint where the item was planned or completed

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Epic or Functional Area User Story or Requirement Tracking ID Priority Planned Sprint Acceptance Basis

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
This section should describe the structure and rationale of the backlog, not list every issue in full. Detailed issue-level evidence belongs in the project board, sprint appendices, or repository records.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Sprint Structure
docs.text: Use this section to explain how the project work was distributed across Sprint 0 and the succeeding development sprints.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Describe the following:

  • the purpose of Sprint 0 in preparing the repositories, backlog, architecture, and validation plan
  • the number of development sprints used in the project
  • the normal activities performed in each sprint, such as planning, execution, review, and retrospective
  • how the team maintained at least three asynchronous progress updates per week and one synchronous weekly progress check-in facilitated by the Research Coordinator
  • how sprint outputs were documented in the appendices and repositories

docs.title: Sprint 0 Expectations
docs.text:
Sprint 0 should establish the project's working foundation before feature implementation begins. Depending on the project scope, this usually includes:

  • repository initialization and branching workflow setup
  • initial product backlog creation and prioritization
  • architecture, logic flow, and data schema drafting
  • validation planning and Definition of Done agreement
  • assignment of initial issues, milestones, or project board columns

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Sprint Main Objective Typical Outputs Tracking Evidence Sprint 0 Establish project foundation and planning artifacts backlog, architecture draft, data schema draft, repository setup, Sprint 0 records project board, setup commits, Sprint 0 appendix Sprint 1 Sprint 2 Sprint 3 Final Sprint

docs.title: Short Worked Example
docs.text:
If the team uses story points during Sprint Planning, add a brief note showing how sprint load and completed work were interpreted.

Example:

Sprint Planned Points Completed Points Velocity Interpretation Sprint 1 10 8 8 two planned stories were deferred to Sprint 2 Sprint 2 9 10 10 one small carryover story was completed together with the planned sprint work Sprint 3 9 9 9 sprint scope matched the team's recent delivery pattern

In the narrative, this may be written as follows:

The team initially planned 10 story points for Sprint 1 but completed only 8 points because two backlog items required additional revision. Using the first three implementation sprints, the team observed a working velocity range of 8 to 10 points. This pattern was then used to keep later sprint commitments realistic and aligned with the approved MVP.

Use this kind of example only when story points were actually tracked in the project. If the team did not use story points, describe sprint scope using the backlog items completed, deferred work, and the reasons for any adjustment.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
In THS 101, this section may describe the planned sprint structure in future tense. In THS 102, revise it to reflect the actual sprint sequence and outputs completed by the team.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Definition of Done
docs.text:
This section should define the minimum conditions that a backlog item must satisfy before the team reports it as complete during a sprint review.

Use the institutional baseline below, then add project-specific quality checks when needed.

Treat the items in the minimum Definition of Done as required unless the team has a documented approved exception. For items marked "when applicable," the team should state in this section why the item applies or does not apply to the project.

docs.title: Institutional Minimum Definition of Done
docs.text:
A backlog item should normally be counted as complete only when:

  • the work has been committed to the correct repository
  • a pull request has been created and reviewed according to the approved workflow
  • the related acceptance criteria have been satisfied
  • relevant testing or verification has been completed successfully
  • CI checks pass when applicable
  • related documentation has been updated
  • evidence is available for the next sprint review

docs.title: Project-Specific Additions
docs.text:
The team may extend the Definition of Done with additional requirements such as:

  • code review by a domain specialist or designated lead
  • updated diagrams, screenshots, or release notes
  • security checks for sensitive modules
  • dataset verification or model evaluation for analytics projects
  • deployment verification for hosted systems

docs.title: Recommended Table Format
docs.text:
DoD Item Required for All Stories Notes Work committed to repository Yes Identify the branch or pull request pattern used by the team. Pull request reviewed Yes Reference CODEOWNERS or the agreed review rule. Acceptance criteria satisfied Yes Link to the user story, issue, or checklist. Testing completed Yes State the type of testing expected for the story. CI checks passed when applicable Yes Use N/A only with justification. Documentation updated Yes Include manuscript, README, or appendix updates when relevant. Extra project-specific condition No Add rows as needed.

If N/A is used for a minimum item, identify the approval basis in the notes column.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
This section should describe the actual quality gate used by the team, not a generic definition copied from Scrum references.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text: This section presents a reference system architecture for a typical BSCS research project that delivers a web-based or mobile-supported application. The diagram below is intentionally written as a reusable baseline: students should replace the module names with the actual services, actors, and deployment components used by their study.

docs.title: Architectural Overview
docs.text:
The reference architecture is organized into five major parts:

  • User roles, which represent the people who directly interact with the system
  • Client layer, which contains the user interface for data entry, dashboards, reports, and login flows
  • Core application services, which enforce the business rules and execute the main project logic
  • Data management layer, which stores operational records, generated reports, and uploaded files
  • Deployment and integrations, which support hosting, notifications, logging, and external services

This layered arrangement keeps presentation, processing, and persistence concerns separate. That separation improves maintainability, makes testing easier, and allows the project to evolve without tightly coupling every feature to a single module.

docs.title: 1. User Roles
docs.text: The architecture assumes three common actors: students or respondents, faculty users such as Advisers or Faculty Panel members, and administrators. In an actual research implementation, these roles may be renamed depending on the domain. For example, a healthcare project may use patients and clinicians, while an inventory project may use staff and managers.

docs.title: 2. Client Layer
docs.text:
The client layer is responsible for collecting input and presenting system output. It may be implemented as a browser-based interface, a mobile application, or a hybrid frontend. Typical client responsibilities include:

  • User authentication
  • Form submission
  • Record browsing and search
  • Dashboard and report viewing
  • Feedback and validation prompts

docs.title: 3. Core Application Services
docs.text:
The core application layer contains the main processing logic of the project. In a completed research project, this is the layer where the study's algorithm, workflow engine, decision rules, or analytics module should be placed. Typical services include:

  • Authentication and access control
  • Business rules and validation
  • Project-specific processing or classification logic
  • Report generation
  • Notification handling
  • Audit trail recording

docs.title: 4. Data Management Layer
docs.text: The data layer stores the information needed by the system during normal operation. This commonly includes structured records in a relational database and unstructured files such as attachments, exports, or generated documents. Backup and recovery mechanisms should also be described here if they are part of the system scope.

docs.title: 5. Deployment and Integrations
docs.text: The final layer describes where the system runs and what outside services it depends on. Depending on the approved scope, this may include a cloud host, on-premise server, email gateway, SMS provider, or third-party API.

docs.title: Data Flow Summary
docs.text:
The expected interaction flow is as follows:

  1. A user submits a request through the client interface.
  2. The client forwards the request to the application layer.
  3. The application layer validates the request, applies business rules, and executes the required process.
  4. Processed data is saved in the database or file storage.
  5. The application may trigger notifications, reporting, or external integrations.
  6. The result is returned to the client for display to the user.

docs.title: Adaptation Guide
docs.text:
Before final submission, revise this section so it matches the actual research project. At minimum, replace the generic labels in the figure and text with the following project-specific details:

  • Actual system users
  • Actual frontend technology
  • Actual backend framework or processing module
  • Actual database or storage platform
  • Actual external services and deployment target

If the research project includes a machine learning model, optimization algorithm, or decision support component, it should be explicitly named under the core application services subsection.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Logic Flow
docs.text: This section describes the end-to-end operational flow of the proposed system. While the system architecture explains how the major components are organized, the logic flow explains how data moves through the application during actual use. The sequence below is written as a reusable template for a typical research system and should be revised to reflect the project's real workflow.

docs.title: General Processing Flow
docs.text:
1. The user opens the system and accesses the login page or landing interface.
  2. The system verifies the user's identity and determines the appropriate role and permissions.
  3. The user selects a feature or module, such as data entry, monitoring, evaluation, reporting, or administration.
  4. The client interface collects the required input values from the user.
  5. The application validates the submitted data for completeness, format, and business-rule compliance.
  6. If the input is valid, the system executes the main processing logic of the selected module.
  7. The processed output is stored in the database or file repository when persistence is required.
  8. The system generates a response, such as a confirmation message, updated dashboard, computed result, or report.
  9. If needed, the system triggers notifications, logging, or external service calls.
  10. The final output is presented to the user through the interface.

docs.title: Relationship to Stored Data
docs.text:
When the project uses persistent storage, the logic flow should be consistent with the data schema presented in the next section. In most systems, the workflow includes a mix of read, create, update, and report-generation actions such as:

  • creating a new transaction, submission, or core record from user input
  • updating record status after validation, review, approval, or processing
  • reading related detail rows, reference records, or historical entries during processing
  • writing generated results, logs, or notifications after the main logic completes

This connection between process steps and stored records helps show that the architecture, workflow, and data design describe the same system rather than separate diagrams.

docs.title: Module-Level Logic
docs.text: The workflow of most research systems can be divided into four recurring phases:

docs.title: 1. Input Phase
docs.text: The system captures raw data from forms, uploaded files, or user selections. At this stage, the system should ensure that required fields are present and that invalid values are rejected before they reach the processing layer.

docs.title: 2. Validation Phase
docs.text:
Before business logic is executed, the application checks the submitted data against system rules. These rules may include:

  • Required-field validation
  • Allowed-value checking
  • Duplicate-record checking
  • Access-permission verification
  • File-type and file-size restrictions

docs.title: 3. Processing Phase
docs.text:
Once validation succeeds, the project-specific logic is executed. Depending on the research topic, this may include:

  • Rule-based decision making
  • Statistical computation
  • Classification or prediction
  • Scheduling or prioritization
  • Search and retrieval
  • Report generation

This is the core stage where the main contribution of the research project should be visible.

docs.title: 4. Output Phase
docs.text:
The result of the processing stage is then displayed or exported. Common outputs include:

  • On-screen summaries
  • Computed recommendations
  • Status updates
  • Charts and reports
  • Downloadable files
  • Notifications or alerts

docs.title: Exception and Error Handling Flow
docs.text:
The logic flow should also account for non-ideal scenarios. A complete research system should define what happens when:

  • A user enters incomplete or invalid data
  • Authentication fails
  • A requested record does not exist
  • A database operation is unsuccessful
  • An external service is unavailable

In these cases, the system should display a clear message, log the event if necessary, and return the user to a recoverable state without corrupting stored data.

docs.title: Suggested Customization for Final Version
docs.text:
Before final submission, replace the generic flow above with the actual step-by-step process of the proposed system. The final logic flow should explicitly name:

  • The system actors
  • The input data used by each module
  • The algorithm or rule applied during processing
  • The generated outputs
  • The conditions for success or error states

If the study includes a decision-support model, machine learning workflow, or optimization process, the exact sequence of that logic should be described here in detail.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Data Schema
docs.text:
This section describes how persistent data is organized in the proposed system and how that structure supports the workflow described in the previous sections. If the project uses a relational database, this section should present the major entities, their important attributes, and the relationships that support the system's core transactions. If the project instead uses document storage, files, or other structured records, the same section may be adapted into a data structure view.

The figure below provides a reusable baseline entity relationship diagram. Replace it with the project's actual schema before final submission.

docs.title: Data Design Overview
docs.text:
The data schema should identify the records that must be stored so the system can authenticate users, process transactions, retain historical data, and generate reports or outputs required by the study. At minimum, the final manuscript should show:

  • The major entities, tables, or data objects used by the system
  • The key attributes that affect the system logic
  • The primary relationships among those entities
  • The constraints or validation rules that preserve data integrity

For most BSCS projects, the stored data can be grouped into the following categories:

  • User and access-control records
  • Core operational or transaction records
  • Reference or configuration records
  • Generated outputs, logs, or archived records when these are part of the project scope

docs.title: Suggested Entity Documentation
docs.text:
The final manuscript should replace the generic examples below with the actual entities used by the project.

Entity / Table Purpose in the System Important Attributes Notes on Constraints Users Stores account and identity information for authorized users user_id, name, email, password_hash, role, status Email should be unique; role should map to allowed permissions Profiles or Actors Stores additional information tied to a specific user type such as student, client, staff, or evaluator profile_id, user_id, contact_details, assigned_unit One profile record may depend on one user account Core Records Stores the main domain transaction handled by the system record_id, title, category, created_at, status Required fields and status values should be validated Record Details Stores supporting values or sub-items attached to the core record detail_id, record_id, attribute_name, attribute_value Often linked through a one-to-many relationship Reports or Results Stores generated outputs, summaries, recommendations, or computed results result_id, record_id, output_value, generated_at May be derived data and may depend on successful processing Audit Logs Stores significant system actions for traceability when logging is included in the scope log_id, user_id, action, timestamp Useful for monitoring, review, and security tracking

docs.title: Relationship Structure
docs.text: The data schema should clearly show how records relate to one another. Common relationship patterns in a research system include:

docs.title: 1. One-to-One Relationships
docs.text: One-to-one relationships are used when one record extends another record without duplication. For example, a user account may have one corresponding profile record that stores role-specific details.

docs.title: 2. One-to-Many Relationships
docs.text: One-to-many relationships are the most common pattern in transaction-based systems. A single user may create many records, and a single core record may contain many detail rows, comments, or status updates.

docs.title: 3. Many-to-Many Relationships
docs.text: Many-to-many relationships should be represented through an associative table when a record can be linked to multiple items and each item can also belong to multiple records. Examples include projects assigned to many members, records tagged with many categories, or users granted multiple permissions.

docs.title: Constraints and Data Integrity
docs.text:
The manuscript should also identify the rules that keep stored data consistent and usable. Depending on the project, these constraints may include:

  • Primary keys for uniquely identifying each record
  • Foreign keys for maintaining valid references between entities
  • Unique constraints for fields such as usernames, email addresses, or control numbers
  • Required fields that prevent incomplete transactions from being stored
  • Status values or enumerations that enforce valid workflow states
  • Timestamps for creation, modification, approval, or archival events

If the system includes deletion or archival behavior, the final manuscript should clarify whether records are permanently deleted, soft deleted, or retained for audit purposes.

docs.title: Alignment with System Workflow
docs.text:
The data schema should not be treated as a standalone database diagram. It should directly support the logic flow and architecture described earlier in Chapter 3. In most projects:

  • User tables support authentication, authorization, and role-based access
  • Core transaction tables support the primary workflow of the system
  • Detail or junction tables support multi-step processing, assignments, or categorization
  • Results tables support generated outputs, dashboards, and reports
  • Logs or history tables support traceability, monitoring, or validation evidence

When writing the final version of this section, explain how the stored records move through the system from data entry to processing to output generation.

The final manuscript should make it easy to map the workflow to the stored data. For example, an input step typically creates a new core record, a validation step may reject or update submitted values, a processing step may produce detail rows or derived results, and an output step may read those saved values for dashboards, reports, or notifications.

docs.title: When a Full ER Diagram Is Not Needed
docs.text:
If the approved project does not use persistent storage, this section may be revised into a lighter data structure discussion instead of a full entity relationship diagram. In that case, describe:

  • The main data objects handled in memory or in files
  • The format of those objects such as JSON, CSV, or model input structures
  • The rules that govern how those objects are validated and transformed
  • How those data structures support the system's main workflow

The final content should still make clear how the project organizes information needed by the application.

docs.title: Final Customization Notes
docs.text:
Before final submission, revise this section so it matches the actual project by replacing the generic placeholders with:

  • The actual entity or table names
  • The actual attributes used by the project
  • The actual relationships and cardinalities
  • The actual constraints relevant to the implementation
  • A short explanation of how the schema supports the system features and validation activities

If the project includes analytics, scoring, scheduling, recommendation, or machine learning outputs, the schema should identify where the input data, derived values, and final results are stored.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Validation Plan
docs.text: This section presents the plan for evaluating whether the developed system meets its intended objectives, functional requirements, and quality targets. The validation plan should show how the proponents will gather evidence that the system is correct, usable, reliable, and appropriate for the identified users.

docs.title: Validation Objectives
docs.text:
The validation activities should confirm the following:

  • The system features work according to the stated requirements
  • The generated outputs are correct and consistent
  • The system is usable by the intended users
  • The application performs acceptably under normal operating conditions
  • Defects discovered during testing are documented and resolved before final demonstration

docs.title: Validation Approach
docs.text: The standard validation strategy combines technical testing and user-centered evaluation. Teams should treat the sections below as the default validation structure unless an approved exception has been documented.

docs.title: 1. Functional Validation
docs.text:
Functional validation verifies that each feature behaves as expected. This may include:

  • Account login and role-based access
  • Data encoding and editing
  • Search, filtering, and retrieval
  • Report generation
  • Export or print functions
  • Notifications and workflow transitions

For each feature, define the test input, expected behavior, actual result, and status of the test case.

docs.title: 2. Usability Evaluation
docs.text:
Usability evaluation measures whether the target users can effectively use the system. A common approach is to ask representative users to perform tasks in the system and then answer a structured questionnaire, such as the System Usability Scale (SUS), a Likert-scale instrument, or a school-approved evaluation form.

For user-facing systems, this section should identify the actual user-validation instrument that will be used. If SUS or SEQ will not be used, the approved alternative should be stated explicitly.

The final manuscript should state:

  • The number of respondents
  • The profile of respondents
  • The evaluation instrument used
  • The scoring interpretation
  • The resulting usability score or rating

docs.title: 3. Performance and Reliability Checks
docs.text:
If the project includes measurable performance, reliability, accuracy, or repeatability concerns, include the relevant technical checks here.

  • Response time during common transactions
  • Accuracy of generated results
  • Successful handling of repeated requests
  • Stability during continuous use
  • Proper data saving and retrieval

These checks do not need to be industrial-scale performance tests, but they should be sufficient to show that the prototype works reliably under the study's intended scope.

docs.title: 4. Security and Data Integrity Checks
docs.text:
For projects that store user information or sensitive records, validation should also verify that:

  • Unauthorized users cannot access restricted modules
  • Input validation prevents invalid or unsafe entries
  • User actions are restricted based on role
  • Stored data remains consistent after create, update, and delete operations

docs.title: Standard Validation Matrix
docs.text:
The table below should be completed and tailored when preparing the final validation evidence.

Validation Area Method Participants / Basis Expected Outcome Functional correctness Test cases Developers / proponents All critical features pass Usability Survey or evaluation form Target users Acceptable usability score Accuracy of outputs Result comparison Manual checking or baseline rules Outputs match expected results Reliability Repeated execution Proponents Stable behavior across repeated runs Security / access control Role-based tests Proponents / admin tester Unauthorized access is blocked

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
The study should define clear criteria for considering the system acceptable. At minimum, the acceptance criteria should include the following:

  • All core features pass functional testing
  • No critical defects remain unresolved before final defense
  • User evaluation results meet the minimum acceptable score required by the institution
  • The system produces correct outputs for the defined test cases
  • The deployed prototype can be demonstrated successfully from start to finish

docs.title: Documentation of Results
docs.text:
All validation evidence should be summarized in Chapter 4. Supporting materials may be placed in the appendices, such as:

  • Test case tables
  • Evaluation forms
  • Survey summaries
  • Screenshots of outputs
  • Error logs or bug-fix summaries

docs.title: Final Customization Notes
docs.text:
Before final submission, tailor this validation plan to the actual research project by specifying:

  • The exact testing instruments used
  • The exact number and type of evaluators
  • The scoring or interpretation method
  • The success thresholds approved by the Adviser or research manual
  • Any additional validation required by the study, such as model accuracy, expert review, or comparative analysis

Use this chapter to present the strongest implementation and validation evidence produced during the project.

docs.title: Scrum Execution
docs.text:
Use this section to report how the team executed the approved Scrum plan from Sprint 0 through the final implementation sprint.

This section is a recurring sprint narrative area under Epic 4 - User Story 1. Update it alongside the sprint appendices so each completed sprint remains traceable in the manuscript.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following:

  • the number of sprints executed and the normal sprint duration
  • the sprint goal for each sprint
  • the major backlog items completed
  • the planned and completed story points when the project tracked sprint velocity
  • important carryover or deferred work
  • key implementation decisions made during the sprint
  • repository evidence such as issues, pull requests, release branches, or tags
  • blockers, revisions, or scope adjustments and how the team responded

docs.title: Sprint 0
docs.text: Describe how the team established the repositories, backlog, architecture direction, validation plan, and project controls.

docs.title: Sprint 1 and Later Sprints
docs.text:
For each sprint, report:

Item Details Sprint Goal Covered Dates Completed Stories or Issues Planned Points and Actual Velocity Important Evidence Blockers or Scope Changes Review Outcome

docs.title: Traceability Reminder
docs.text:
The narrative in this section should remain consistent with the product backlog, sprint appendices, and repository history. If a planned item was deferred, descoped, or replaced, state that directly so the implementation record remains traceable.

If the project used story points, report the planned sprint load, completed points, and resulting velocity using the same basis described in the sprint planning records. Count only accepted and completed stories in the reported velocity.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Write this section in past tense in THS 102. Focus on actual execution evidence rather than generic Scrum definitions.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Features and Outputs
docs.text:
Use this section to present the implemented features, major outputs, and concrete evidence that the approved MVP was delivered.

This section is a recurring sprint reporting area under Epic 4 - User Story 2. Keep the narrative aligned with the per-sprint appendix evidence so completed outputs and deferred scope remain traceable.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
For each major feature or module, report:

  • the requirement, story, or objective addressed
  • what was implemented
  • the output produced by the system
  • the evidence used to verify the feature
  • whether the implemented output satisfied its acceptance criteria or expected behavior
  • any remaining limitation relevant to interpretation

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Feature or Output Related Requirement or Story What Was Implemented Evidence Acceptance Check Notes or Limitation

docs.title: Figures and Screenshots
docs.text:
Use figures only when they provide clear evidence such as:

  • interface screenshots of major workflows
  • output reports or generated results
  • architecture or logic artifacts referenced in the implementation discussion

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Do not repeat the full methodology here. Focus on what was actually built and what the artifact produced.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: CI/CD Results
docs.text: Use this section to present the build, workflow, and pipeline evidence that supported repository reliability during the implemented sprints.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following when CI/CD or equivalent repository automation is part of the approved workflow:

  • the workflow or pipeline names used by the project
  • the checks performed, such as build verification, linting, automated tests, static analysis, or deployment validation
  • the relevant sprint, release, branch, tag, or milestone covered by the reported runs
  • the outcome of the important runs, including failed runs that caused revisions when relevant
  • how the pipeline evidence supports the implemented outputs or validation activities

If CI/CD was not part of the approved workflow, state that directly and identify the approved replacement verification basis.

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Workflow or Pipeline Sprint or Release Covered Checks Performed Latest Result Important Failed Run or Revision Evidence Reference

docs.title: Interpretation Guide
docs.text:
Use a short narrative after the table to explain:

  • what the pipeline verified
  • whether the repository passed the required checks consistently
  • what issues were discovered and how they were resolved
  • what the pipeline does not verify so claims remain proportional to the evidence

docs.title: Traceability Reminder
docs.text: Keep this section consistent with repository history, workflow-run records, sprint appendices, and the validation plan. If a workflow changed mid-project, explain when the change happened and why.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Write this section in past tense in THS 102. Focus on actual workflow evidence, not a generic explanation of CI/CD.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: System Testing Results
docs.text: Use this section to report the executed system tests that verified whether the implemented artifact satisfied the approved functional requirements.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Report the following for the major tests that matter to the MVP:

  • the feature, workflow, or requirement being tested
  • the test case or validation activity executed
  • the expected result
  • the actual result observed during execution
  • the pass or fail outcome
  • the evidence reference, such as screenshots, logs, test reports, or repository-linked artifacts

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Feature or Requirement Test Case or Scenario Expected Result Actual Result Status Evidence Reference

docs.title: Optional Supporting Details
docs.text:
Add short notes when needed for:

  • the test environment or execution conditions
  • important defects found and how they were resolved
  • deferred or partially passing behavior that affected scope interpretation

docs.title: Traceability Reminder
docs.text: The reported tests should remain traceable to approved requirements, user stories, backlog items, and sprint evidence. If a requirement was not tested in this section, explain where its verification evidence appears instead.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Do not turn this section into a raw dump of all test logs. Present the major executed tests and the outcomes needed to support the validation narrative.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Code Coverage Results
docs.text: Use this section when the approved workflow includes automated tests with coverage reporting.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following when code coverage tooling is part of the project workflow:

  • the coverage tool or report source used by the project
  • the sprint, module, release, or branch covered by the reported metric
  • the reported coverage value or range
  • the scope of the covered tests
  • important uncovered areas that affect interpretation

If code coverage was not part of the approved workflow, state that directly and identify the approved replacement evidence used instead.

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Coverage Scope Tool or Report Source Reported Coverage Test Scope Covered Important Uncovered Area Evidence Reference

docs.title: Interpretation Guide
docs.text:
After the table, explain:

  • how the coverage evidence supports automated verification
  • whether the reported coverage is adequate for the approved workflow
  • what important limitations remain because of uncovered code paths or excluded modules

docs.title: Traceability Reminder
docs.text: Keep this section aligned with the automated testing workflow, CI/CD evidence, and sprint appendices. If the coverage basis changed across sprints, make that change explicit.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Coverage is supporting evidence, not the full validation basis by itself. Keep the claims limited to what the coverage report actually shows.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Security Verification Results
docs.text: Use this section to report security-related verification evidence when the approved workflow includes dependency checks, secret scanning, code scanning, or other security review activities.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following when security verification was part of the project workflow:

  • the security checks or scanning tools used by the project
  • the sprint, release, repository area, or deployment stage covered by the reported checks
  • the important findings, including whether they were resolved, accepted, or remained open
  • the practical meaning of the findings for repository hygiene, deployment readiness, or technical limitations
  • what the reported security checks did not cover

If formal security verification was not part of the approved workflow, state that directly and identify the approved alternative assurance basis.

docs.title: Standard Table
docs.text: Security Check or Tool Scope Covered Result Summary Resolved or Remaining Issue Interpretation Evidence Reference

docs.title: Interpretation Guide
docs.text:
Use a short narrative after the table to explain:

  • what the reported security evidence actually verified
  • what important risks were found or ruled out
  • what unresolved issues, accepted risks, or workflow limitations remained at the time of reporting

docs.title: Traceability Reminder
docs.text: Keep this section consistent with repository alerts, workflow history, sprint appendices, and the validation plan. Do not imply a full security assessment unless the project actually performed one.

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Keep the discussion proportional to the evidence gathered. This section should show practical verification results, not generic security theory.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Validation Results
docs.text:
Use this section to present the final validation evidence for the system and explain whether the project objectives were met.

This section synthesizes the strongest evidence gathered across the recurring result areas in Chapter 4. User Stories 1 to 6 may be updated per sprint, while this final validation section consolidates the approved evidence basis used for interpretation in Chapter 5.

docs.title: Minimum Required Evidence
docs.text:
At minimum, this section should show:

  • documented functional validation of the core MVP features
  • the main technical verification evidence used by the project
  • user validation evidence for user-facing systems or the approved alternative metric for non-user-facing systems
  • a short explanation of whether the project met its validation basis

docs.title: Standard Additional Evidence
docs.text:
Beyond the minimum required evidence, report the strongest additional evidence gathered during validation, such as:

  • functional test results
  • automated testing or CI evidence when applicable
  • code coverage when coverage tooling is used
  • security verification or dependency scan results when applicable
  • SUS and SEQ findings for user-facing systems when applicable
  • other approved technical metrics such as accuracy, latency, error rate, or expert evaluation

docs.title: 1. Functional Validation Summary
docs.text: Validation Item Expected Result Actual Result Status Evidence Reference

docs.title: 2. Technical Verification Summary
docs.text: Evidence Type Result Interpretation CI build or verification run Automated tests Code coverage when applicable Security or dependency checks when applicable

docs.title: 3. Automation and Repository Evidence
docs.text:
When the project uses repository automation, summarize the checks that supported build stability and traceability. This may include:

  • CI or workflow run status for the final release or major sprint outputs
  • the checks performed by the pipeline such as build verification, test execution, linting, or static analysis
  • failed runs or pipeline revisions that materially affected the project

docs.title: 4. User Validation Summary
docs.text: Metric Result Minimum Target or Basis Interpretation SUS 68 when SUS is used SEQ Task-level usability evidence UAT completion rate Team-defined task basis

docs.title: 5. Interpretation
docs.text:
Explain clearly:

  • whether the approved MVP met its objectives
  • what evidence was strongest
  • what limitations remained after validation
  • how the findings affect deployment, turnover, or future work

docs.title: 6. Optional Evidence Areas
docs.text:
If applicable to the approved workflow, add short summaries for:

  • code coverage results and what areas remained uncovered
  • security scanning or dependency verification findings
  • performance, accuracy, or reliability measures that support the project's technical claims

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
If a metric was not applicable, say so directly, identify the replacement evidence used instead, and make sure the approved exception is traceable in the validation plan or review record.

docs.title: Chapter 5 Discussion
docs.text: This chapter interprets the project findings, states the resulting conclusions, identifies limitations, and records actionable recommendations.

docs.title: Chapter Use
docs.text:
Use this chapter to connect the reported evidence to the research questions and to explain what the results mean for future use or improvement.

Next ➡️

docs.title: Summary of Key Findings
docs.text: Use this section to summarize the most important findings of the study based on the evidence already presented in Chapter 4.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Summarize the following:

  • the main requirement or problem addressed by the project under RQ1
  • the most important implementation outcome under RQ2
  • the strongest validation result or evidence point under RQ3
  • the overall finding for RQ1, RQ2, and RQ3 at a high level without introducing new evidence

docs.title: Suggested Summary Pattern
docs.text:
Keep the summary short and evidence-based. A typical structure is:

  • one short paragraph summarizing the problem, requirements basis, or approved scope under RQ1
  • one short paragraph summarizing the implemented artifact and major output under RQ2
  • one short paragraph summarizing the strongest validation result under RQ3

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
This section should synthesize the results, not repeat every table or screenshot. Keep the claims tied to evidence already reported in the manuscript and limit the section to a concise restatement of findings.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Conclusions Based on Validation
docs.text: Use this section to state the project's conclusions based on the reported results and explain whether the approved research objectives were achieved.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Discuss the following:

  • the conclusion for RQ1 based on the documented requirements, scope, or approved design basis
  • the conclusion for RQ2 based on the implemented artifact and completed outputs
  • the conclusion for RQ3 based on the validation evidence
  • the specific result, metric, or finding that supports each conclusion
  • whether any objective remained partially achieved or constrained by limitations

docs.title: Suggested Conclusion Table
docs.text: Research Question or Objective Evidence Basis Conclusion RQ1 / requirements objective RQ2 / implementation objective RQ3 / validation objective

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Do not present raw data here for the first time. This section should derive conclusions directly from Chapter 4 evidence and keep the statements factual, traceable, and free of new claims.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text: Use this section to state the real limitations of the study and the developed system.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Identify limitations such as:

  • restricted MVP scope or approved out-of-scope boundaries
  • limited respondent count or evaluation setting
  • unavailable integrations or datasets
  • prototype-only deployment conditions
  • algorithmic, performance, or usability constraints still present at final defense

docs.title: Distinguish Two Kinds of Limits
docs.text:
This section should separate:

  • scope boundaries that were intentionally excluded from the approved MVP
  • actual limitations or constraints that affected implementation, validation, or interpretation of results

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Limitations should be specific and honest. Do not hide important constraints, but do distinguish between approved scope boundaries and unexpected implementation problems.

⬅️ Previous | Next ➡️

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text: Use this section to present actionable next steps based on the findings and limitations of the study.

docs.title: Required Coverage
docs.text:
Recommendations may include:

  • improvements for the next system version
  • additional validation or deployment work
  • technical refinements for the core logic or architecture
  • broader rollout, integration, or scaling considerations
  • future research directions that remain outside the present MVP

At minimum, the final manuscript should provide two to three concrete recommendations that follow directly from the reported findings or limitations.

docs.title: Recommendation Quality Check
docs.text:
Each recommendation should be:

  • specific enough that a future team can act on it
  • connected to a reported limitation, unmet target, or improvement opportunity
  • realistic within the project's domain and technical context

docs.title: Writing Reminder
docs.text:
Recommendations should follow directly from the findings and limitations. Avoid adding unrelated ideas that were never motivated by the results.

docs.title: Sprint 0 Review and Sprint 1 Planning
docs.text:
Use this appendix as the formal working record for the closeout of Sprint 0 and the preparation of Sprint 1. It must be completed by the team together with the Adviser and Research Coordinator after Sprint 0 outputs have been reviewed.

This is a routine sprint-governance record. The Student Team owns the sprint work at Level 0, while the Adviser and Research Coordinator provide Level 1 delivery-support review. The Teacher and Faculty Panel may inspect this record later as gate evidence, but they are not the normal Sprint 0 approvers.

docs.text:
Use the standard SUS scoring method when computing the Total Contribution and SUS Score columns.
